mistakes.summary: Letter exceeds 220 words and gets penalised under C4 (Conciseness).
mistakes.example_wrong: I am writing to refer Mr Holm, a 55-year-old sales manager. He has been suffering from daytime sleepiness for several months and his wife has noticed loud snoring and apnoeic episodes during the night. He works long hours in his sales role and his wife is now very concerned. He has had two near-miss incidents while driving... [letter continues to 245 words with extensive social-history padding]
mistakes.example_right: I am writing to refer Mr Holm, a 55-year-old sales manager, who has been provisionally diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnoea. Your assessment and management would be appreciated. [letter remains within 180-200 words by removing repetition and social padding]

mistakes.summary: Letter under 160 words and graded as under-developed (C2 Content tank).
mistakes.example_wrong: I am writing to refer Mr Holm. He has sleep apnoea. Please assess and arrange treatment. [letter ends at 95 words with no past history, no examination findings, no specific request].
mistakes.example_right: I am writing to refer Mr Holm, a 55-year-old sales manager, who has been provisionally diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnoea. [...] His Epworth score is 18 and STOP-BANG 6 of 8. I would be grateful if you could arrange overnight polysomnography. [letter reaches 185 words with proper history, examination and request paragraphs].

mistakes.summary: Writing a discharge as if it were a referral — opens with 'I am writing to refer' for a discharge case.
mistakes.example_wrong: I am writing to refer Mrs Carter, a 62-year-old retired teacher, who was discharged today following pneumonia.
mistakes.example_right: I am writing to inform you that Mrs Carter was discharged today following a five-day admission for community-acquired pneumonia.

mistakes.summary: Treating a response as a new referral — restating the question instead of answering it.
mistakes.example_wrong: I am writing to refer Mr Okonkwo, a 61-year-old, who has type 2 diabetes that is not well controlled on metformin and dapagliflozin. [opener invents a referral that doesn't exist].
mistakes.example_right: Thank you for your letter of 19 May 2026 regarding Mr Okonkwo's diabetes management. I am writing in response to your enquiry about insulin titration. He attended the pharmacist-led diabetes clinic today and the following plan has been agreed...

mistakes.summary: Including full social history in a discharge to GP (violates SC-020).
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Holm was discharged today after pneumonia. He smokes 20 cigarettes a day, drinks 30 units of alcohol per week, lives alone in a council flat and works as a self-employed plumber.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm was discharged today after pneumonia. He has been advised on smoking cessation, which is relevant to his ongoing respiratory care. [Other social details omitted.]

mistakes.summary: Missing a key clinical finding — e.g., not mentioning Epworth score in an OSA referral.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Holm has daytime sleepiness and his wife noticed snoring. Please assess.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm has six-month daytime sleepiness, witnessed apnoeic episodes, an Epworth Sleepiness Scale of 18 (severe) and STOP-BANG of 6 of 8 (high risk). Clinical examination revealed a BMI of 32 and neck circumference of 44 cm.

mistakes.summary: Mis-attributing observations to the wrong source (e.g., listing patient-reported symptoms as examination findings).
mistakes.example_wrong: Clinical examination revealed daytime sleepiness, witnessed apnoeic episodes and morning headaches.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm reports daytime sleepiness, witnessed apnoeic episodes and morning headaches. Clinical examination revealed a BMI of 32, neck circumference of 44 cm and Mallampati class III.

mistakes.summary: Using 'the patient' instead of the name or pronoun.
mistakes.example_wrong: The patient has been suffering from daytime sleepiness. The patient also reports morning headaches. The patient's wife is concerned.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm has been suffering from daytime sleepiness. He also reports morning headaches. His wife is concerned.

mistakes.summary: Re-introducing the patient's name every sentence instead of using pronouns.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Holm has had sleepiness for six months. Mr Holm reports falling asleep at meetings. Mr Holm has had two near-misses driving. Mr Holm's wife is concerned.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm has had sleepiness for six months. He reports falling asleep at meetings. He has had two near-miss incidents while driving. His wife is concerned.

mistakes.summary: Using non-approved linkers (Moreover, Furthermore, Therefore, Thus, sentence-initial However).
mistakes.example_wrong: However, Mr Holm denied chest pain. Moreover, he has had two near-miss incidents. Furthermore, his Epworth score is 18.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm denied chest pain; however, he has had two near-miss incidents while driving. In addition, his Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is 18.

mistakes.summary: Using contractions in formal-clinical register ('don't', 'isn't', 'it's').
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Holm hasn't been sleeping well and his wife's concerned because he's been having near-misses.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm has not been sleeping well and his wife is concerned because he has had near-miss incidents.

mistakes.summary: Wrong conditional in the request: 'I would be grateful if you would assess' instead of 'if you could'.
mistakes.example_wrong: I would be grateful if you would assess Mr Holm at your earliest convenience.
mistakes.example_right: I would be grateful if you could assess Mr Holm at your earliest convenience.

mistakes.summary: Tense shift: mixing past simple, present perfect and present simple within the same paragraph without a 'Today,' anchor.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Holm presents today with sleepiness. He had been suffering for six months. His wife has noted snoring. He denies chest pain.
mistakes.example_right: Today, Mr Holm presented for review. He has been suffering from sleepiness for six months. His wife has noted witnessed apnoeic episodes. Please note, he denied chest pain.

mistakes.summary: Subject-verb agreement errors with collective nouns or compound subjects.
mistakes.example_wrong: His observations was stable. The team are reviewing the case daily.
mistakes.example_right: His observations were stable. The team is reviewing the case daily.

mistakes.summary: Using American spelling and vocabulary (color, organize, edema, Tylenol, ER).
mistakes.example_wrong: Mrs Carter was seen in the ER with edema and given a Tylenol shot. We have organized follow-up.
mistakes.example_right: Mrs Carter was assessed in A&E with bilateral oedema and given paracetamol. We have organised follow-up.

mistakes.summary: Lay-language slips: 'tummy', 'pee', 'feeling sick', 'a bit of pain'.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Holm has been having a bit of tummy pain and feeling sick. He's also been peeing more often.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm has been experiencing epigastric pain and nausea. He has also reported increased urinary frequency.

mistakes.summary: Re: line malformed — wrong capitalisation, age format or missing word 'aged'.
mistakes.example_wrong:
Re: MR HOLM, 55 YEARS OLD
RE: Mr Holm (55)
Re: Mr Holm, Aged: 55
mistakes.example_right: Re: Mr Thomas Holm, aged 55

mistakes.summary: Missing the canonical closing sentence and/or the standardised sign-off.
mistakes.example_wrong:
...I would be grateful if you could review Mr Holm.
Kind regards,
Dr Ahmed
mistakes.example_right:
...I would be grateful if you could arrange overnight polysomnography.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed

mistakes.summary: Pharmacy letters using medical referral conventions ('contact me' close, no explicit request in opener).
mistakes.example_wrong: Dear Dr Khan, I am writing about Mr Williams' medications. He is taking warfarin and naproxen. Please look at this. If you need anything else, please do not hesitate to contact me.
mistakes.example_right: Dear Dr Khan, I am writing to alert you to a high-risk co-prescription of warfarin and naproxen identified at clinical check today, and to request review of Mr Williams' analgesia. [...] If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact the pharmacy.

mistakes.summary: Nursing letters straying into medical-style differential diagnoses or prescribing decisions outside scope.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Adetola may have an underlying chronic venous insufficiency with possible superficial venous reflux; I would recommend duplex scan and possibly endovenous laser ablation. He may also need long-term penicillin-V prophylaxis.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Adetola has confirmed chronic venous insufficiency and a healing left venous leg ulcer. The community nursing team is asked to continue 4-layer compression dressings twice weekly and to monitor for signs of infection. Re-referral to tissue viability is requested if the wound deteriorates or fails to heal within 6 weeks.

mistakes.summary: Sign-off uses 'Yours faithfully' when the recipient is named (should be 'Yours sincerely').
mistakes.example_wrong:
Dear Dr Park,
...
Yours faithfully,
Dr Sarah Ahmed
mistakes.example_right:
Dear Dr Park,
...
Yours sincerely,
Dr Sarah Ahmed

mistakes.summary: Missing blank line between closing courtesy and 'Yours sincerely,' sign-off — layout penalty under C4.
mistakes.example_wrong:
If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.
Yours sincerely,
Dr Sarah Ahmed
mistakes.example_right:
If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed

mistakes.summary: Bullet points used in letter body when task instruction prohibits or convention discourages.
mistakes.example_wrong:
Today, Mr Holm presented with:
- daytime sleepiness
- witnessed apnoeic episodes
- two near-miss driving incidents
mistakes.example_right: Today, Mr Holm presented with daytime sleepiness, witnessed apnoeic episodes and two near-miss driving incidents in the past eight weeks.

mistakes.summary: Using 'kindly' which sounds patronising in formal British clinical letters.
mistakes.example_wrong: Kindly review Mr Holm at your earliest convenience.
mistakes.example_right: I would be grateful if you could review Mr Holm at your earliest convenience.

mistakes.summary: Imperative voice in the request paragraph instead of the canonical polite stem.
mistakes.example_wrong: Arrange overnight polysomnography for Mr Holm and start him on CPAP if indicated.
mistakes.example_right: I would be grateful if you could arrange overnight polysomnography and consider commencement of continuous positive airway pressure therapy if indicated.

mistakes.summary: 'Less' used for countable items (should be 'fewer').
mistakes.example_wrong: He has had less episodes of angina since starting bisoprolol.
mistakes.example_right: He has had fewer episodes of angina since starting bisoprolol.

mistakes.summary: 'Continual' confused with 'continuous' for uninterrupted therapy.
mistakes.example_wrong: He has been on continual oxygen therapy at 2 L per minute throughout admission.
mistakes.example_right: He has been on continuous oxygen therapy at 2 L per minute throughout admission.

mistakes.summary: 'Affect' (verb) confused with 'effect' (usually noun).
mistakes.example_wrong: The new medication has effected her sleep pattern and the prognosis is good.
mistakes.example_right: The new medication has affected her sleep pattern and the prognosis is good.

mistakes.summary: Drug brand name used instead of British generic.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mrs Carter has been prescribed Lipitor 40 mg nocte and Ventolin as required.
mistakes.example_right: Mrs Carter has been prescribed atorvastatin 40 mg nightly and salbutamol as required.

mistakes.summary: American date format (month-first) instead of British (day-first).
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Holm was admitted on 05/26/2026 and discharged on 05/30/2026.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm was admitted on 26 May 2026 and discharged on 30 May 2026.

mistakes.summary: Dose abbreviations (OD, BD, TDS, QDS) used in letter prose where expansion is required.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mrs Carter was discharged on amoxicillin 500mg TDS for 7d and paracetamol 1g QDS PRN.
mistakes.example_right: Mrs Carter was discharged on amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for seven days and paracetamol 1 g four times daily as required.

mistakes.summary: Failing to include the date of the most recent investigation, leaving the receiver unable to interpret the value.
mistakes.example_wrong: His most recent INR was 2.6 (within target range).
mistakes.example_right: His most recent INR on 18 May 2026 was 2.6, within the target range of 2 to 3.

mistakes.summary: Including the patient's direct quotation instead of using reported speech.
mistakes.example_wrong: The patient said: 'I feel really tired all the time and I can't go on like this.'
mistakes.example_right: Mr Holm reports persistent fatigue that he describes as limiting his daily activities.

mistakes.summary: Misclassifying urgency — writing a routine referral when the case notes call for urgent.
mistakes.example_wrong: I am writing to refer Mr Patel, a 47-year-old taxi driver, who has had chest pain since this morning. Your assessment in due course would be appreciated.
mistakes.example_right: I am writing to urgently refer Mr Patel, a 47-year-old taxi driver, who presents with a 90-minute history of crushing central chest pain and electrocardiographic changes suggestive of inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Immediate cardiology assessment is required.

mistakes.summary: Treating a transfer as a discharge — opening with 'I am writing to inform you that... was discharged' when the patient is being moved to another specialist unit, not going home.
mistakes.example_wrong: I am writing to inform you that Mr Mason was discharged today following decompensated heart failure.
mistakes.example_right: I am writing to transfer the care of Mr Mason to your service following failure of ward-based diuresis for decompensated heart failure, requesting acceptance for advanced management.

mistakes.summary: Pharmacy intervention letter that buries the request in the body instead of stating it explicitly in the opening.
mistakes.example_wrong: Dear Dr Adeyemi, I am writing to inform you about Mr Williams' recent prescription. He is currently taking warfarin and naproxen. We noticed an interaction. After discussion the naproxen was withheld. Please review when convenient.
mistakes.example_right: Dear Dr Adeyemi, I am writing to alert you to a high-risk co-prescription of warfarin and naproxen identified at clinical check today, and to request review of Mr Williams' analgesia. Your prompt input would be appreciated.

mistakes.summary: Doctor writing detailed nursing observations (Bristol stool chart, fluid balance interval data) that belong in nursing handover, not in a medical letter.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Mason's fluid balance showed input 2.2 L (oral 1.6 L, IV 0.6 L), output 1.4 L (urine 1.2 L, drain 200 mL), net +800 mL with Bristol stool type 5 once on day 2.
mistakes.example_right: Mr Mason has remained in significant positive fluid balance with persistent peripheral and pulmonary oedema despite intravenous furosemide.

mistakes.summary: Pharmacist describing detailed physical examination findings beyond medication-related observations.
mistakes.example_wrong: On cardiovascular examination, Mr Williams' apex beat was undisplaced, his second heart sound was normal and there were no murmurs.
mistakes.example_right: Clinical examination at the pharmacy revealed no overt bruising or pallor, with two small bleeding points noted on the gums and otherwise intact oral mucosa.

mistakes.summary: Re: line written in all uppercase or omitting 'aged' — violates SC-014 conventions.
mistakes.example_wrong:
RE: MR HOLM (55Y/O)
Re: MR HOLM 55 YEARS
Re: Mr Holm, age 55
mistakes.example_right: Re: Mr Thomas Holm, aged 55

mistakes.summary: Subjective adjectives without clinical quantification — 'severe pain', 'a lot of swelling', 'horrible cough'.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mrs Carter had a horrible cough with a lot of green sputum and severe pleuritic pain on the right side of her chest.
mistakes.example_right: Mrs Carter had a productive cough with greenish sputum and pleuritic right-sided chest pain rated 7 out of 10 on the numerical rating scale.

mistakes.summary: Padded opening paragraph that introduces medications and history before stating the purpose — wastes the most-graded sentence on filler.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mr Holm is a 55-year-old gentleman known to this practice with a long-standing history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes for which he takes amlodipine and metformin. He has been our patient for many years. I would like to discuss with you his sleep symptoms.
mistakes.example_right: I am writing to refer Mr Holm, a 55-year-old sales manager, who has been provisionally diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnoea. Your assessment and management would be appreciated.

mistakes.summary: Treating a discharge home as if it were a referral to a new specialist, requesting investigation rather than continuation of care.
mistakes.example_wrong: I am writing to refer Mrs Carter, a 62-year-old retired teacher, who has had pneumonia. I would be grateful if you could arrange chest imaging and respiratory review.
mistakes.example_right: I am writing to inform you that Mrs Carter was discharged today following a seven-day admission for community-acquired pneumonia. It would be appreciated if you could arrange a repeat chest X-ray in six weeks to confirm radiological resolution.

mistakes.summary: Reporting examination findings without the unit of measurement — leaves the recipient unable to interpret severity.
mistakes.example_wrong: Clinical examination revealed a blood pressure of 142, a pulse of 78 and oxygen saturations of 96.
mistakes.example_right: Clinical examination revealed a blood pressure of 142 over 88 mmHg, a regular pulse of 78 beats per minute and oxygen saturations of 96 percent on room air.

mistakes.summary: Quoting a drug dose without the frequency, route or duration — clinically incomplete and unsafe for handover.
mistakes.example_wrong: He has been started on amoxicillin 500 mg and ramipril.
mistakes.example_right: He has been started on oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for seven days and ramipril 5 mg daily, continued indefinitely.

mistakes.summary: Including 'apparently', 'allegedly' or 'reportedly' when reporting the patient's history — these words cast doubt and read as disrespectful.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mrs Pearson apparently had a fall last week and allegedly hit her head, although she reportedly cannot remember the details.
mistakes.example_right: Mrs Pearson sustained a fall last week with possible head impact; she is unable to recall the details of the event.

mistakes.summary: Dangling participle in clinical prose — modifier attached to the wrong subject.
mistakes.example_wrong: Walking into the surgery, his chest pain was severe.
mistakes.example_right: On walking into the surgery, Mr Patel reported that his chest pain was severe.

mistakes.summary: Using 'patient denies' as a stative present tense when reporting a past consultation — should be past simple per the today-paragraph anchor.
mistakes.example_wrong: Today, Mr Holm presents for review and denies chest pain.
mistakes.example_right: Today, Mr Holm presented for review. Please note, he denied chest pain.

mistakes.summary: Recipient address and date block missing or jumbled — undermines the professional appearance and costs C3/C4 marks.
mistakes.example_wrong:
26-05-2026
To Helena Park
Dear Helena,

Re: Tom Holm 55
mistakes.example_right:
Dr Helena Park
Consultant in Sleep Medicine
City General Hospital

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Park,

Re: Mr Thomas Holm, aged 55

mistakes.summary: Nursing handover that recommends specific antibiotic agents or doses — outside scope; the prescribing decision belongs to the medical team.
mistakes.example_wrong: The community team should commence oral co-amoxiclav 625 mg three times daily for seven days and add metronidazole if the wound smells offensive.
mistakes.example_right: The wound shows signs of localised infection. Re-referral to the medical team or GP for antibiotic prescribing is recommended; in the interim the wound has been swabbed and the dressing changed.

mistakes.summary: Pharmacist offering a definitive medical diagnosis instead of flagging concerns and deferring to prescriber.
mistakes.example_wrong: Mrs Murphy has SIADH secondary to citalopram and requires fluid restriction; sertraline should be substituted.
mistakes.example_right: Mrs Murphy's recent symptoms and serum sodium of 128 mmol/L are suggestive of SSRI-related hyponatraemia following the recent citalopram uptitration; review and consideration of dose reduction or alternative agent is requested.
